name: A-Haven Townhouse
blurb: A Victorian townhouse guesthouse on Ferry Road, a short walk into Leith proper — full Scottish breakfast included, free parking, and rooms that are simple rather than shabby (~CAD 140–175/night).
signature: ask for a room at the back — Ferry Road carries traffic

name: Nira Caledonia
blurb: A Georgian townhouse on the New Town edge of Stockbridge — 26 individually decorated rooms, a genuinely good breakfast, and quiet residential-street charm steps from St Stephen Street (~CAD 325–435/night).
signature: book early for May — Friday nights jump well above the weekday rate

name: Fingal
blurb: A decommissioned lighthouse-tender ship, permanently docked in Leith and converted into 23 cabins named after Stevenson lighthouses — the birthday-splurge night that becomes part of the trip's story (~CAD 685–795+/night).
signature: one night aboard for the birthday — book months ahead, 23 cabins only

name: Fishers Leith
blurb: The original Fishers, at the foot of a 17th-century windmill on The Shore — over twenty years of simply-cooked Scottish fish, and a smoked-fish platter that's a meal in itself (~CAD 33–59/mains).
signature: the smoked-fish platter — salmon, trout, mackerel, rollmops, oysters, langoustine

name: Dulse Leith
blurb: AA Rosette seafood on Constitution Street — the menu changes with what local fishermen bring in that morning, and the hands-on seafood boil is built for lingering (~CAD 72–100+/pers).
signature: the seafood boil — mussels, langoustine, crab, £78 for two, book ahead

name: The Fishmarket Newhaven
blurb: A Victorian fishmonger's counter turned takeaway hatch at Newhaven's little harbour — fish and chips cooked to order, oysters at harbourside tables, and a straight view to Fife past the boats (~CAD 22–35/pers).
signature: fish & chips from the hatch, eaten on the harbour wall

name: Twelve Triangles — Brunswick Street
blurb: A scratch bakery working entirely in cold-fermented sourdough — no additives, everything hand-shaped, and doughnut flavours worth planning a morning around (~CAD 6–15/pers).
signature: the doughnuts — coffee custard, pink grapefruit ricotta — before 10am

name: Roseleaf Bar Café
blurb: A family-run Leith fixture since 2007 on Sandport Place — hearty Scottish comfort food, all-day brunch, and cocktails served in mismatched teapots (~CAD 18–35/pers).
signature: cocktails in a teapot — Somebody Feed Phil drank here too

name: Dean Village & Water of Leith Walkway
blurb: A former milling village on a bend of the Water of Leith, utterly silent at 7am — Well Court's clock tower, the view from Dean Bridge, and a footpath upstream that feels like leaving the city entirely.
signature: go at 7am, then walk the river path to Stockbridge

name: Portobello Beach
blurb: Edinburgh's own beach — a proper stretch of sand facing the Firth of Forth, a promenade lined with cafés, and a year-round wild-swimming crowd that treats the North Sea as a perfectly reasonable place to be.
signature: golden hour on the promenade, then the sauna

name: Soul Water Sauna — Portobello
blurb: A wood-fired mobile sauna right on the Portobello promenade, steps from the North Sea — the honest version of a spa day: heat, cold plunge, repeat, host stoking the fire the whole time (~CAD 24–46/session).
signature: sauna, North Sea plunge, repeat — bring a swimsuit and two towels

name: Stockbridge Market
blurb: Forty-plus independent stalls on Saunders Street by the Water of Leith, every Sunday all year — Lanark Blue cheese, Scottish charcuterie, East Lothian veg, hot food, and dog owners outnumbering tourists.
signature: Sunday 10am–4pm — it runs on May 23, 2027

name: Newhaven Harbour
blurb: A small working fishing harbour with its own lighthouse and a straight sightline across the Forth to Fife — walk the pier before or after lunch at the Fishmarket hatch, boats coming and going the whole time.
signature: morning for the boats, late afternoon for the light on the Forth

name: Arthur's Seat — at sunrise
blurb: The one classic sight worth setting an alarm for — an extinct volcano inside the city, empty at dawn, with the whole of Edinburgh and the Forth laid out below while the tour buses are still parked.
signature: summit 15 minutes before sunrise (~4h45 in late May) — headlamp, wind layer

name: Port of Leith Distillery
blurb: Scotland's first vertical distillery — nine storeys of working whisky-making on Leith's waterfront — where a 90-minute tour ends with your own miniature bottle filled and a dram on the 8th-floor bar over the docks (~CAD 55/pers).
signature: the 90-minute tour + a dram over the working port — book ahead, timed entry

name: Tregortha Guesthouse
blurb: A Victorian stone villa on the hill above Tighnabruaich, four ensuite rooms, breakfast in a conservatory looking straight down the Kyles of Bute — the honest budget pick with the £200-a-night view (~CAD 165–215/night).
signature: the conservatory breakfast over the Kyles

name: Kames Hotel
blurb: A family-run hotel on the shore at Kames, a mile from Tighnabruaich — sea-view rooms over the Kyles of Bute, a proper bar-restaurant downstairs, and a full Scottish breakfast that name-checks Loch Fyne kippers (~CAD 195–275/night).
signature: ask for a sea-view room over the Kyles

name: Portavadie — Loch Fyne
blurb: A converted 1970s oil-rig yard turned architect-designed marina resort — Scotland's biggest heated outdoor infinity pool sits at the edge of Loch Fyne, so you swim looking at open water. One or two nights of assumed holiday (~CAD 295–480/night, cottage).
signature: the loch-edge infinity pool — day-spa access works even without a room

name: Loch Fyne Oyster Bar & Deli
blurb: The shed at Cairndow that put Loch Fyne oysters on the map in 1978 — independently owned again, natives and rocks shucked to order at the counter, smoked salmon done on site (~CAD 28–65/pers).
signature: a dozen shucked to order, eaten twenty metres from the water they grew in

name: The Oystercatcher — Otter Ferry
blurb: A bar-restaurant literally on the Loch Fyne shore, twenty minutes up the B8000 from the base — fresh local seafood, daily specials, tables angled straight at the water, under new owners who haven't lost the plot (~CAD 28–55/pers).
signature: dinner facing the loch — closed Mondays, call ahead

name: Skipness Seafood Cabin
blurb: A wooden cabin, a few picnic tables, the sea and a 13th-century castle ruin as your dining room — dressed crab and whole langoustines, family-run for decades (~CAD 15–37/dish).
signature: dressed crab at a picnic table under the castle — closed Saturdays, seasonal

name: The Red Herring — Tarbert
blurb: Tarbert harbour's current seafood spot — opened 2024 in the old fish-bar premises and already the local default: fresh fish off the boats outside, honest prices, no ceremony (~CAD 19–37/pers).
signature: the day-boat fish, landed a hundred metres away

name: The Kilberry Inn
blurb: A converted croft at the end of a single-track road on Kintyre's west coast — the coast's one Bib Gourmand (2026). Taken over by the Kilberry Estate and CLOSED for a full renovation at last check (July 2026), reopening date not announced — verify before building a night around it.
signature: closed for renovation as of mid-2026, reopening TBC — check before travelling

name: Ostel Bay (Kilbride Bay)
blurb: A crescent of sand facing due south to Arran, four miles from Kames — widely called Cowal's best beach, reached by a layby at Kilbride Farm and a short walk through the dunes. The coach tours never come.
signature: low tide widens the sand — morning light comes in gold toward Arran

name: Kyles of Bute Viewpoint
blurb: A signed layby on the A8003 above Tighnabruaich with the single best view on this coast — three narrow sea channels wrapping the north end of Bute — and you'll likely have it to yourself.
signature: evening light — the sun drops behind Bute and the channels go silver

name: The Secret Coast loop
blurb: A half-day single-track loop around the southern tip of Cowal — Millhouse, Ardlamont Point, the Ostel Bay turn-off, then back up the B8000 along Loch Fyne through Otter Ferry. Seals, oystercatchers, and not another car for long stretches.
signature: practice your passing-place waves — it's the local handshake

name: Portavadie–Tarbert ferry (CalMac)
blurb: A 25-minute crossing of Loch Fyne that turns the whole Kintyre seafood run into an easy day trip — about 11 sailings a day, turn-up-and-go, pay on board (~CAD 19–28 car + driver).
signature: the gateway to Tarbert langoustines and the Skipness crab

name: Isle of Bute Distillery (Oyster Gin)
blurb: Scotland's shortest ferry crossing (5 minutes, hourly, no booking) hops you onto Bute for the distillery that puts actual Loch Fyne oyster shells in the still — a briny, maritime gin built to pair with seafood (~CAD 42/tasting).
signature: Oyster Gin tasting — the seafood theme, distilled

name: Inveraray Castle & Jail
blurb: History as backdrop, not the point: the Duke of Argyll's fairy-tale Georgian pile and the genuinely well-done jail museum next door — go at opening, then make the real event the oyster-bar lunch on the same road home (~CAD 28–37/site).
signature: at opening, before the coaches — busiest 11am–2pm

name: Crinan Canal & Kilmartin Glen
blurb: A slower day out: yachts locking through at the Crinan sea lock, lunch at the Crinan Seafood Bar, then eight hundred prehistoric monuments within six miles of Kilmartin on the drive home — without either asking to be the main event.
signature: the Seafood Bar, not Lock 16 (groups only in season) — Kilmartin as backdrop

name: Ceol na Mara Guest House
blurb: Tarbert's top-rated guest house — five-star grading, a short walk from the ferry pier, run with the kind of care that keeps it at #1 of the village's B&Bs year after year (~CAD 185–230/night).
signature: book 3–6 months ahead — Harris beds go fast for early June

name: Hotel Hebrides
blurb: Tarbert's old harbour hotel refurbished into a 21-room boutique on the ferry pier — the Pierhouse restaurant and The Mote bar (locals, live Gaelic sessions) mean eating and drinking well without moving the car (~CAD 365+/night).
signature: the practical comfort pick — Luskentyre is 15 minutes away

name: The Rock House — Borve Lodge Estate
blurb: A single-storey stone-and-glass retreat built for two, positioned to watch the Atlantic roll straight onto Borve beach — one bay over from Luskentyre, total privacy, Taransay offshore. The 50th-birthday splurge (~CAD 425+/night, self-catering).
signature: the view that started the trip — books 6–12 months ahead; The Broch is the fallback

name: North Harbour Bistro — Scalpay
blurb: Right on Scalpay's harbour, ten minutes past Tarbert over the bridge — the menu is whatever George Lavery's creel boats landed that morning: langoustine, scallops, crab, sometimes within the hour (~CAD 28–55/pers).
signature: today's weather, translated into shellfish — closed Sundays, call ahead

name: Croft36 — Northton
blurb: An honesty-box shed, not a restaurant — Julie and Steve Olley have baked bread and cooked seafood here since 2010. Langoustine soup, lobster butteries, homemade tarts, cash or PayPal in the box (~CAD 7–22/item).
signature: the lobster buttery — arrive before noon, it sells out

name: Isle of Harris Brewery & Taproom
blurb: The old Anchorage building on Leverburgh pier, reborn in 2024 as the island's brewery — twelve house beers, a deli-bottle-shop, and a fish-forward menu whose langoustines are named in honour of the restaurant that fed this pier for twenty years (~CAD 20–28/plate).
signature: the 'Anchorage langoustines' with a house beer, west-facing over the Sound — closed Sundays

name: Scarista House
blurb: A Georgian manse turned six-room hotel where dinner is a daily-changing, no-choice set menu — island fish, shellfish, machair lamb, home-baked everything — candlelit, facing three miles of shell-sand beach (~CAD 100–130/pers).
signature: the dining pilgrimage of the trip — open to non-residents, book well ahead

name: Luskentyre & Seilebost
blurb: Three miles of white shell sand and water that runs through every shade of turquoise, backed by machair and the silhouette of Taransay — at low tide the two beaches merge into one enormous flat threaded with tidal channels.
signature: mid-to-low tide for the colour; early morning for nobody — watch the channels as the tide turns

name: Hushinish & the B887
blurb: Twelve miles of single-track switchbacks from Tarbert, sheep contesting right of way the whole time — ending at a sheltered white beach good for swimming, looking straight at the uninhabited island of Scarp.
signature: the drive is the price and the beach is the reward — late afternoon light on the machair

name: The Golden Road (Bays of Harris)
blurb: The east coast — the exact opposite of the west's beaches: a slow, twisting 1897 road through terrain geologists compare to the moon, past inlets too small for proper harbours. Stop at Skoon in Geocrab for coffee.
signature: allow two hours to actually stop — the appeal is the rock and the crofts, not a golden hour

name: St Clement's Church, Rodel
blurb: A late-15th-century church at the island's southern tip holding the carved tomb of Alexander MacLeod — 'the finest late medieval sculpture in the Western Isles' — with sheep grazing between the leaning gravestones outside.
signature: morning light through the small windows onto the tomb — almost never crowded

name: Scalpay & Eilean Glas Lighthouse
blurb: Bridge-connected Scalpay, then the waymarked (boggy) circular walk to the 1824 Stevenson lighthouse — about 13 km round trip, with a tiny coffee shop in the keepers' cluster and open water beyond. Shorter version: just the village and the bistro.
signature: start by mid-morning for the full loop — reward: coffee at the light, nothing else for miles

name: Isle of Harris Distillery
blurb: The 'social distillery' in Tarbert, founded to strengthen the island's economy — a 75-minute tour covering the sugar-kelp gin and The Hearach, the first single malt ever distilled, matured and bottled on Harris. Peat fire always burning in the visitor centre.
signature: tours Mon–Fri 10am/12pm/2pm, closed Sundays — book ahead

name: St Kilda day trip or wildlife boat
blurb: The big-ticket option: a full-day open-Atlantic sail from Leverburgh to St Kilda, the abandoned edge-of-the-world archipelago (~CAD 575). Lower commitment: local wildlife trips past seal skerries and sea-eagle perches, sometimes to the puffin colonies of the Shiants (~CAD 260).
signature: weather-dependent — book weeks ahead and keep a buffer day

name: Polcraig Guest House
blurb: Family-run B&B a short walk from Lochinver harbour — plain, spotless, unfussy rooms and a proper cooked breakfast; quietly good for decades rather than freshly styled for Instagram (~CAD 130–160/night single).
signature: the kind of place that's been good for decades — contact directly for rates

name: Davar Guest House
blurb: Four-star B&B right on the harbour — the king room looks straight across Lochinver bay to Suilven and Cul Mor, which is most of the reason to book it. Three rooms only (~CAD 250+/night).
signature: Suilven from the bed — three rooms, books out fast for June

name: Suilven View Pod (Waterloo House)
blurb: A hillside pod above Lochinver built around one view: Suilven, framed from the bed and from the two-person hot tub. Self-catering, sleeps two — now let through Waterloo House after a change of operator; price on enquiry (~CAD 250 range historically).
signature: the pod changed hands in 2024-25 — enquire directly (07595 727355); Inver Lodge is the formal fallback

name: Peet's Restaurant
blurb: Harbourside in Lochinver, family-run since 2014, terrace over the water — fresh scallops, creel langoustines and the seafood platter that carries the weight of every evening you don't drive to Ullapool (~CAD 37–65/pers).
signature: the seafood platter — closed Wednesdays, reserve ahead, don't wing it

name: An Cala Café
blurb: Set in the old Fishermen's Mission by the Lochinver harbour — breakfast, seafood lunches, afternoon tea and home baking; the reliable daytime anchor in a village that runs on one dinner restaurant (~CAD 15–37/pers).
signature: the harbour breakfast — evening meals some weeks only, call ahead

name: Lochinver Larder & Pie Shop
blurb: Forty years of independent pie-making on Main Street — the Venison & Cranberry took Scotland's Best Meat Pie at the British Pie Awards 2022. Deli counter for pies-to-go, café tables for sit-in (~CAD 11–22/pers).
signature: the venison & cranberry pie — Tue–Sat 10–4, plan the pie-run

name: The Seafood Shack — Ullapool
blurb: Ullapool's cult seafood counter — ten years as a trailer, upgraded April 2026 to a permanent timber servery on the same spot. Still takeaway, still no bookings, still built entirely around what the boats landed (~CAD 22–46/pers).
signature: langoustines in hot garlic butter, eaten on the harbour wall — no indoor seating, that's the point

name: Am Fuaran Bar — Altandhu
blurb: A real coastal local at the far end of the Coigach peninsula, beer garden looking straight at the Summer Isles — creel langoustines gathered by a relative of the owners, in lemon-ginger or garlic butter (~CAD 28–65/pers).
signature: langoustines with the Summer Isles for a backdrop — pair with the Stac Pollaidh day

name: Achmelvich Bay
blurb: Caribbean-white sand ten minutes from Lochinver, water that goes genuinely turquoise on a calm day, gneiss headlands framing the bay and Suilven's profile behind — the most photographed water in Assynt for good reason.
signature: before 9:30am or after 6pm — the convoys own 11-to-4, the light owns the rest

name: Clachtoll Beach & Broch
blurb: Gently sloping white sand plus a genuine Iron Age broch, excavated 2017–2023 after a fiery collapse two thousand years ago preserved it almost intact — an easy flat walk from the car park, otters and dolphins offshore.
signature: the broch stonework in early-evening light — quieter than Achmelvich all day

name: Clashnessie Bay & Falls
blurb: One of the few Assynt beaches facing north, with a fraction of the visitors — and a 20-minute walk from the car park to a 15-metre waterfall dropping into a boulder plunge pool. The real hidden-beach pick.
signature: the falls almost nobody walks to — best after rain

name: The Drumbeg road (B869)
blurb: Twenty-two miles of single-track from Lochinver to Kylesku via Drumbeg and Nedd — blind summits, hairpins, no barriers, sea on one side and Assynt's bare mountains on the other. Rated harder than Applecross, which is why the motorhomes skip it.
signature: morning, compact car, reverse into passing places without complaint — that's the game

name: Wailing Widow Falls
blurb: A 30-metre cascade off Loch na Gainmhich, three hundred yards from a roadside car park between Loch Assynt and the Kylesku Bridge — one of the easiest real-waterfall payoffs in Sutherland.
signature: ten minutes return — the natural stop at the north end of the Drumbeg loop

name: Stac Pollaidh
blurb: Coigach's little mountain — a short, punchy two-to-three-hour circuit to a saddle with half of Assynt to the north and the Summer Isles to the south. The east summit needs no scrambling; leave the true top to the scramblers.
signature: by 9am or after 4pm (small car park) — the low sun over the Summer Isles is the better show

name: Bone Caves & Ardvreck Castle
blurb: A marked walk to four limestone caves where lynx, reindeer and polar-bear remains were dug out of the Ice Age — bring a torch. Ardvreck's ruined tower on Loch Assynt is the five-minute photo stop down the road, with Quinag doing the real work behind it.
signature: history as backdrop, exactly where it belongs — early or late for an empty foreground

name: Falls of Kirkaig & the Suilven view
blurb: From the Inverkirkaig car park, a wooded two-mile path to a 20-metre waterfall — the short, honest version of Suilven without committing to the mountain. Push one more kilometre to Fionn Loch for the classic sugar-loaf reveal.
signature: morning light for the Fionn Loch reveal — steep final drop to the falls, good shoes

name: Summer Isles boat trip
blurb: Two operators, two piers: the twelve-passenger 'Isabella' from Achiltibuie (three sailings a day, Mon–Sat) or the longer wildlife run from Ullapool harbour — seals on the skerries, dolphins, porpoises, the occasional eagle (~CAD 83–88/pers).
signature: book ahead — twelve seats sell out; pair with Stac Pollaidh and an Am Fuaran dinner
